steps.title: Identify what the claim requires as evidence
steps.content: The student claims the film is "not a precise retelling" but rather "a largely imagined but informed representation." Valid evidence must establish two things simultaneously: (1) Powers lacked detailed historical facts — so the film could not be a precise retelling — and (2) he researched the figures thoroughly — so the imaginative rendering was still informed.

steps.title: Eliminate each choice and confirm the answer
steps.content:
• ""When Powers learned of the meeting, he initially planned to write a much longer work about its four famous participants rather than focusing on the meeting itself."": discusses Powers's original scope, not his approach to historical accuracy or research depth. ✗
  • '"One Night in Miami... received numerous awards and nominations, including an Academy Award nomination for Powers for Best Adapted Screenplay."': addresses critical reception, not the nature of the representation. ✗
  • '"Powers has described One Night in Miami... as the story of four friends encouraging and supporting one another while engaged in a crucial political debate about how best to achieve equality for Black people in the United States."': describes thematic content but says nothing about whether the portrayal is historically precise or imaginatively constructed. ✗
  • ""Powers could find only the most superficial historical details about the meeting, so he read extensively about the four individuals and their thinking at the time in an effort to portray what might have happened between them."": shows the account could not be a precise retelling (sparse historical record) and was nonetheless informed (extensive research into the individuals), directly satisfying both parts of the claim. ✓
That matches the correct answer — D is correct.
